title: Synchronous code — the call stack
instruction:
JavaScript executes code on a **call stack** — last in, first out (LIFO).

When a function is called, it's pushed onto the stack. When it returns, it's popped off.

Run this and understand the order.
successMessage: The stack: a is called, b is called inside a, c is called inside b. c returns, b continues, b returns, a continues. Always completes in the order functions were entered.

title: setTimeout — the macrotask queue
instruction:
When `setTimeout(fn, 0)` is called:
1. The timer is registered with the browser/Node.js
2. JS moves on immediately (doesn't wait!)
3. When the current synchronous code finishes, the event loop checks the **macrotask queue**
4. The callback runs

So even `setTimeout(fn, 0)` runs AFTER all synchronous code.

Preddict the output before running.
successMessage: Sync first (1, 4, 5), then macrotasks (2, then 3). setTimeout(fn, 0) doesn't mean immediate — it means 'run after current sync code finishes, minimum 0ms wait'.

title: Promises — the microtask queue
instruction:
Promise callbacks run in the **microtask queue** — which is checked BEFORE the macrotask queue.

Microtask queue processing:
1. Run all synchronous code
2. Drain the ENTIRE microtask queue
3. Run ONE macrotask (setTimeout callback)
4. Drain microtasks again
5. Repeat

Predict the order.
successMessage: Sync (1, 5) → microtasks (3, 4) → macrotask (2). Promise callbacks ALWAYS run before setTimeout callbacks — this is why async/await code runs predictably.

title: Microtasks run before next macrotask
instruction:
The microtask queue is completely drained between every macrotask.

If a microtask adds another microtask, THAT runs before any macrotask.

This can starve macrotasks if you're not careful.
successMessage: microtask 2 ran before timeout 1 — even though it was created after the timeouts were registered. Microtasks always drain completely before the next macrotask runs.

title: The event loop and await
successMessage: Both A and B ran up to their awaits before sync 2. Then sync 2 ran. Then both continuations ran. await suspends the function, not the thread.

title: queueMicrotask — low-level scheduling
instruction:
`queueMicrotask(fn)` adds a function directly to the microtask queue — without a Promise wrapper.

Faster than `Promise.resolve().then(fn)` — used in performance-critical libraries.
successMessage: queueMicrotask and Promise both queue microtasks — they run in order before the macrotask. queueMicrotask is the low-level API; Promise.then is higher-level.

title: Blocking the event loop
instruction:
Since JavaScript is single-threaded, CPU-intensive synchronous code blocks everything — including timers, incoming HTTP requests, and Promise callbacks.

This is why you should never do heavy computation on the main thread.
successMessage: The timer asked for 100ms but got 300ms — because synchronous code was blocking the event loop. This is why Node.js server performance tanks if you do CPU work on the main thread.

title: Build a task scheduler
instruction:
Final challenge — no starter code.

Build `createScheduler()` that returns:
- `schedule(task, priority)` — schedules a task with priority ('high' = microtask, 'low' = macrotask)
- `flush()` — returns a Promise that resolves when all scheduled tasks run

High priority tasks should always run before low priority ones.

Test with a mix of high and low priority tasks.
hint: High priority: queueMicrotask() or Promise.resolve().then(). Low priority: setTimeout().

codeChecks.message: Use queueMicrotask or Promise.resolve().then for high priority.
Use setTimeout for low priority tasks.

successMessage: A priority task scheduler using microtasks and macrotasks! React's Scheduler package does something similar to split work into high/low priority lanes. Chapter 20 done!
